1: 'I charge you in the presence of God and of Christ Jesus, who is to judge the living and the dead, and by his appearing (epiphaneian) and his kingdom' — the solemn charge formula is the most elaborate in Paul's letters, combining the divine courtroom (God and Christ as witnesses), the eschatological judgment role of Christ (judge of living and dead, cf. Acts 10:42; 1 Peter 4:5), and the two eschatological events (appearing and kingdom) by which the charge is sworn. Swearing by God as witness was a recognized legal and rhetorical form in both Jewish and Roman culture; this charge has the force of a deathbed oath by the world's supreme judge.
2: 'Preach the word (kēryxon ton logon); be ready in season and out of season (eukairōs akairōs)' — the herald-announcement (kēryxon) is the primary mode of apostolic proclamation: not classroom instruction but the public announcement of the king's message. Eukairōs akairōs (in the good moment and in the bad moment, whether convenient or inconvenient) captures the comprehensive, non-opportunistic character of the mandate: the herald does not wait for favorable conditions. The triple command — reprove (elegxon), rebuke (epitimēson), exhort (parakaleson) — with complete patience and teaching covers the full range of pastoral address: diagnosis, correction, and encouragement.
3: 'For the time is coming when people will not endure sound teaching (hygiainousēs didaskalias), but having itching ears (tēn akoēn knēthomenoi) they will accumulate for themselves teachers to suit their own desires' — knēthomenoi tēn akoēn (having itching ears) is a vivid metaphor for the desire to hear pleasant-sounding rather than truthful speech; the ears 'itch' for their preferred kind of stimulation and seek out teachers who will scratch the itch. The accumulation of teachers (episōreuousin didaskalous) — 'heap up' is the literal sense — describes a consumerist approach to religious instruction: choosing teachers by preference rather than by truth.
4: 'They will turn away from listening to the truth and wander off into myths (mythous)' — the trajectory from sound teaching to myths runs through the itching ears of 2 Timothy 4:3: the preference for pleasant-sounding teaching leads eventually to the complete replacement of the Christ-announcement with religious fiction. The mythoi (myths) here echo the myths condemned in 1 Timothy 1:4 and 2 Timothy 4:7; they are the speculative religious elaborations that the false teachers of Ephesus produce. The movement is always the same: from the truth of the person (Christ) to the comfort of the system (myth).
5: 'As for you, be sober-minded (nēphe) in everything, endure suffering (kakopathēson), do the work of an evangelist (ergon euangelistou), fulfill your ministry (tēn diakonian sou plērophorēson)' — four imperatives that define Timothy's countercultural mandate: sobriety (against the intoxication of false excitement), suffering-endurance (the soldier-pattern of v. 2 Timothy 3:2), evangelism (the herald-work of 2 Timothy 4:2), and ministry-completion. The 'evangelist' (euangelistēs) title appears only here and Acts 21:8 (Philip) and Ephesians 4:11 in the NT; it designates the specific role of announcing the good news to those who have not yet heard.
6: 'I am already being poured out as a drink offering (spendomai), and the time of my departure (analyseōs) has come' — the libation metaphor (spendō) refers to the ritual pouring of wine or oil as an offering to the gods; Paul uses it in Philippians 2:17 as a possible metaphor for his martyrdom (if I am being poured out as a drink offering upon the sacrifice and service of your faith). Analysis (departure, release) was a Stoic and Pythagorean term for death as the loosing or release of the soul from the body; Paul's use of the term is almost certainly conscious adoption of the Stoic framework for death as release rather than extinction.
7: 'I have fought the good fight (ton kalon agōna ēgōnismai), I have finished the race (ton dromon teteleka), I have kept the faith (tēn pistin tetērēka)' — the three perfect-tense verbs express completed actions whose effects remain: the fight is fought and the result stands; the race is finished and the finish line is crossed; the faith is kept and its keeping is a present reality. The three athletic-military-depositional metaphors summarize Paul's entire apostolic career: the sustained combat of preaching, the directed course of missionary travel, and the guardianship of the gospel entrusted to him. The perfect tenses convey completed-and-settled accomplishment rather than ongoing processes.
8: 'The crown of righteousness (ho tēs dikaiosynēs stephanos) is stored up for me (apokeitai), which the Lord, the righteous judge (dikaios kritēs), will award to me on that day' — the athletic crown (stephanos) was the victor's wreath at the Greek games (pine at Isthmia, wild olive at Olympia, laurel at Pythia); winning it was the supreme athletic honor. Paul's crown is not a natural prize but a judgment award: the 'righteous judge' (not a sports referee but the divine judge) will award it at the last day. The crown 'is stored up' (apokeitai, is laid aside, awaiting the moment) — the verb is the same as Colossians 1:5 (the hope stored up for you in heaven): the eschatological reward is already secured, waiting for its presentation.
9: 'Do your best to come to me quickly' — the urgency behind the request is the approaching execution. Paul has only Luke with him (2 Timothy 4:11); he needs Timothy's presence and the items listed in 2 Timothy 4:13. The personal request for Timothy's presence sits in tension with the letter's instruction to Timothy to remain at his Ephesian post (1 Timothy 1:3); Paul's need has now become great enough that he calls him to Rome. The specific urgency (quickly, tacheōs) and the reference to getting there before winter (2 Timothy 4:21) suggest a narrow window of opportunity.
10: 'Demas, in love with this present world (agapēsas ton nun aiōna), has deserted me and gone to Thessalonica' — Demas appears as a co-worker in Colossians 4:14 and Phm 24; his desertion is the painful inversion of Paul's ministry-succession theme. The love of 'this present world' (ho nun aiōn) is the Pauline shorthand for the age that is passing away (1 Corinthians 7:31) — the temporal order whose attractions compete with the eschatological hope. Crescens and Titus have gone to Galatia and Dalmatia apparently on legitimate mission assignments; Demas's departure is described in terms of abandonment rather than commission.
11: 'Luke alone is with me. Get Mark and bring him with you, for he is very useful to me for ministry' — Mark's rehabilitation: the same John Mark whose departure from the first missionary journey caused the split between Paul and Barnabas (Acts 15:37-39) is now 'very useful' (euchrēstos). The reconciliation of Paul and Mark is one of the NT's quiet restoration narratives; Colossians 4:10 already commends Mark to the Colossians (Paul's companion) and Phm 24 lists him as a co-worker. Luke's presence alongside Paul for the entire journey (the 'we' sections of Acts include Rome) is consistent with Luke being Paul's physician (Colossians 4:14: Luke the beloved physician).
12: 'Tychicus I have sent to Ephesus' — Tychicus is one of Paul's most trusted letter-carriers; he delivered Colossians (Colossians 4:7-8), Ephesians (Ephesians 6:21-22), and possibly this letter to Timothy at Ephesus. The instruction to bring Mark and the mention of sending Tychicus to Ephesus together suggest that Timothy's presence in Rome is being arranged: Tychicus will serve at Ephesus while Timothy travels to Rome. The apostolic network continues to function as a coordinated system even from prison.
13: 'When you come, bring the cloak (phailonēn) that I left with Carpus at Troas, and the books (ta biblia), and above all the parchments (tas membranas)' — the phailonēs (Latin paenula) was a heavy wool travel cloak; Paul's request for it suggests he is suffering from cold in prison. The biblia (scrolls, papyrus rolls) and membranai (parchments, membrane notebooks) are the specific items valued above all others; the parchments (membranai is the Latin membrana transliterated into Greek) may be Paul's Scripture codices or personal documents. The specificity of the request (Troas, Carpus, the exact items) is the kind of detail that resists pseudepigraphical fabrication.
14: 'Alexander the coppersmith (chalkeus) did me great harm; the Lord will repay him according to his deeds' — a coppersmith (chalkeus) in the Roman world worked with bronze, copper, and related metals; Ephesus had a significant metalworking guild (cf. Demetrius the silversmith, Acts 19:24). Alexander may be the same person as the Alexander of 1 Timothy 1:20 (handed to Satan) or of Acts 19:33; the specific identification as a 'coppersmith' (a professional descriptor) is the kind of detail that suggests a real person from Paul's network. The retributive formula 'the Lord will repay him' (apodōsei autō ho kyrios) echoes Psalms 62:12 and Romans 12:19: divine justice, not human revenge.
15: 'Be on your guard against him yourself, for he strongly opposed our message (logois hēmōn antestē lian)' — Alexander's opposition to 'our message' suggests his hostility was specifically to the gospel proclamation, not merely to Paul personally; he may have been an informant to Roman authorities or an active opponent in the synagogue or legal proceedings. The warning to Timothy to guard himself suggests Alexander's sphere of influence extends to Ephesus where Timothy is stationed; the danger is ongoing, not merely historical.
16: 'At my first defense (apologia) no one came to support me (parestē); all deserted me' — the apologia (defense) before the Roman court was a formal legal hearing; the parestē (stood beside) describes the role of supporting witnesses or character witnesses who would testify for the defendant. Paul's experience of standing alone before the Roman court echoes Psalms 22:11 (there is no one to help) and the Servant of Isaiah 53:7-8 (he was taken away without a fair trial). The phrase 'may it not be charged against them' (mē autois logistheiē) echoes Stephen's final words (Acts 7:60: Lord, do not hold this sin against them).
17: 'But the Lord stood by me (parestē moi ho kyrios) and strengthened me' — in contrast to the human deserters, the Lord is the ultimate support; the divine parestē (stood beside) answers the human failure to stand beside. The strengthening language (enedynamōsen me) echoes 1:7 and the general Pauline confidence that divine power is perfected in weakness (2 Corinthians 12:9-10). The purpose — 'so that through me the message might be fully proclaimed and all the Gentiles might hear it' — frames the last Roman hearing as a missionary opportunity: Paul's courtroom appearance was a platform for gospel proclamation to the Roman officials present.
18: 'The Lord will rescue me from every evil attack and bring me safely into his heavenly kingdom' — the confidence expressed here is not rescue from execution (Paul has already anticipated that in 2 Timothy 4:6-8) but rescue from the evil attack that would compromise his fidelity or destroy his testimony. The heavenly kingdom (hē basileian tēn epouranion) is the destination beyond death — the same eschatological reality that Philippians 3:20-21 describes as the 'commonwealth in heaven' from which the Savior will return. To him be the glory — the doxology that follows is the final theological word of the final Pauline letter.
19: 'Greet Priscilla and Aquila and the household of Onesiphorus' — Priscilla and Aquila appear throughout the Pauline correspondence as one of the most important couple-partnerships in the early mission: at Corinth (Acts 18:2-3; 1 Corinthians 16:19), at Ephesus (Acts 18:18-19, 26), and at Rome (Romans 16:3-5: 'greet Prisca and Aquila, who risked their necks for my life'). Their greeting from Rome to Timothy in Ephesus suggests they may have moved back to Ephesus after the Neronian persecution in Rome. The Onesiphorus household (without Onesiphorus himself) reinforces the probability that Onesiphorus has died.
20: 'Erastus stayed at Corinth, and I left Trophimus, who was ill, at Miletus' — the Erastus who stayed at Corinth may be the Erastus of Romans 16:23 (the city treasurer, oikonomos, of Corinth) or a different associate. A Miletus paving stone inscription reads 'Erastus who is aedile laid this pavement at his own expense' — possibly the same person, though identification is uncertain. Trophimus was an Ephesian Christian (Acts 20:4; 2 Timothy 21:29); his illness and Paul's inability to heal him (despite Paul's healing ministry earlier) is a sobering reminder that apostolic power did not include unlimited miraculous healing on demand.
21: 'Do your best to come before winter' — sea travel in the ancient Mediterranean was effectively suspended from early November to early March (the mare clausum, closed sea); ships did not sail in winter because of the danger (the shipwreck of Acts 27 occurred in late autumn). Paul's request to come before winter sets a concrete deadline: if Timothy does not travel soon, he will not be able to reach Rome until spring, and Paul's execution may come before then. Linus mentioned here is identified by Irenaeus (Adv. Haer. 3.3.3) and Eusebius (HE 3.2) as the first bishop of Rome after Peter and Paul.
22: 'The Lord be with your spirit. Grace be with you all' — the closing benediction shifts from singular ('your spirit,' sou) to plural ('you all,' hymas), indicating the letter, though addressed to Timothy, is intended for reading to the wider Ephesian community (the pattern of the Pauline corpus: the letter to the community leader is simultaneously a letter to the community). The 'grace' (charis) formula is the standard Pauline benediction closing formula, appearing in every Pauline letter; this final instance closes Paul's last surviving letter and is, on the traditional view, his final written word.
